steps.title: Establish the key fact from the passage
steps.content: The passage states that the Round Table first appears in writing in Roman de Brut (1155) and is absent from all earlier texts, including Geoffrey of Monmouth's History. This makes Roman de Brut the documented origin point for any Arthurian Round Table reference.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Geoffrey of Monmouth's accounts of Arthurian legends in his History are more similar overall in content to the accounts in Tom a Lincoln than they are to the accounts in Roman de Brut.': The passage makes no overall content comparison among these three works. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Geoffrey of Monmouth was unaware of stories of the Round Table when composing his History, though historians know that works containing such stories were available to him.': The passage says the Round Table doesn't appear in the History, but never claims such stories were available to Monmouth. ✗
  • Choice C — 'any Arthurian legend that mentions the Round Table likely has a lineage that connects it to Roman de Brut.': Since Roman de Brut is where the Round Table first appears, any later mention must trace back to it—directly supported by the passage. ✓
  • Choice D — 'Roman de Brut is more historically accurate than History, because Tom a Lincoln had not been written when Geoffrey of Monmouth was writing his History.': The passage never compares historical accuracy, and the reasoning about Tom a Lincoln's timing is irrelevant to accuracy. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
